content.text: Every distributed system produces more observability data than it can afford to store indefinitely at full fidelity. Retention tiers solve this by placing data on the cheapest storage class that still satisfies the access latency the use case demands — hot storage for real-time dashboards, warm storage for trend queries, cold storage for compliance evidence — and automating the lifecycle transitions between them.
What It Is

Retention tiering is a data lifecycle strategy that assigns each piece of observability data — distributed traces, structured logs, metrics, and profiling samples — to a storage class based on its age and how quickly it must be retrievable. The most recent data sits in a hot tier backed by fast indexed storage (Elasticsearch, Loki's hot chunk cache, Tempo's SSD backend). As data ages, lifecycle policies automatically move it to progressively slower and cheaper tiers: warm (HDD-backed object storage), cold (infrequent-access buckets or Glacier Instant), and eventually to frozen archive or deletion.

The tier boundaries are defined by two independent variables: access latency tolerance and cost budget. A production on-call engineer needs to query the last 24 hours of logs in under a second; the same query against six-month-old data can tolerate minutes of retrieval time. This latency asymmetry justifies paying 20–50x more per gigabyte for the hot tier, because only a small fraction of the total data volume ever lands there. Cold and frozen tiers accumulate data over years, but the read frequency is low enough that the economics work out dramatically in the operator's favour.

Retention tiers are not the same as sampling. Sampling decides which data to keep at ingest time; tiers decide how long to keep it and at what cost after it has already been kept. Both are complementary strategies: sample to reduce volume at the source, then tier what remains to control long-term storage cost.
Why It Matters

A production agentic AI system running thousands of tool calls per minute can generate hundreds of gigabytes of trace and log data per day. Storing all of that in a full-fidelity, sub-second-queryable index for a year would cost tens of thousands of dollars monthly — and most of that data is never queried after the first 48 hours. Without tiering, organisations face a binary choice: pay the full hot-tier price for everything, or aggressively delete data and lose the ability to investigate historical incidents or satisfy compliance audits.

Compliance requirements add a legal dimension. SOC 2 audit evidence, GDPR processing records, and PCI-DSS log retention mandates each specify minimum retention windows — commonly 90 days for security logs, one year for audit events, and seven years for financial records. A flat retention policy that deletes everything after 30 days violates these minimums. A tiered policy satisfies the compliance floor cheaply by moving aged data to cold storage rather than keeping it in the expensive hot tier.
Benefits
Cost reduction: moving data from hot (~$0.23/GB/month on SSD-backed Elasticsearch) to cold (~$0.004/GB/month on S3 Glacier) achieves a 50–60x cost reduction for aged data with no loss of durability
Compliance satisfaction: cold and frozen tiers provide durable, tamper-evident storage for the years-long windows required by SOC 2, HIPAA, and PCI-DSS without the cost of hot-tier indexing

Hot-tier performance: keeping only recent data in the indexed tier means index sizes stay bounded, shard counts are manageable, and query latency stays low for the queries that matter most to on-call engineers
Incident investigation depth: even though old data lives in slow storage, it is still retrievable for post-incident analysis, legal discovery, or root-cause investigation of recurring problems
Automated lifecycle: policy-driven transitions eliminate manual data management; engineers do not need to remember to archive or purge data
Trade-offs

Retrieval latency: cold-tier queries against Glacier Instant take milliseconds but Glacier Flexible/Deep Archive take hours — investigating a 200-day-old incident becomes a multi-hour blocking operation
Retrieval cost: Glacier charges per-GB retrieval fees in addition to storage fees; a large historical query that retrieves hundreds of gigabytes can incur more cost than months of storage savings

Operational complexity: tiered pipelines require lifecycle policy configuration in each storage system, separate alerting for transition failures, and documentation so engineers know which tier holds which data age range
Compaction delays in Loki: data that has not been compacted on the hot tier cannot transition cleanly to cold; premature transitions can leave incomplete chunks that fail to query

Minimum storage durations: Glacier Instant Retrieval charges a minimum 90-day storage fee even if data is deleted earlier; transitions must account for these minimums to avoid paying more than the hot tier
How It Works

Data enters the observability pipeline through a collector — OpenTelemetry Collector for traces and metrics, Promtail or Alloy for logs. The collector writes to the hot-tier backend, where data is immediately indexed and queryable. In Elasticsearch, this means a write to a time-series data stream whose indices are pinned to the hot node tier. In Loki, ingested chunks are written to an in-memory or disk-backed ingester and then flushed as immutable chunks to the hot object store prefix.

Lifecycle policies run on a schedule and evaluate each data unit against its age. Elasticsearch Index Lifecycle Management (ILM) defines a sequence of phases: hot, warm, cold, frozen, and delete. At each phase boundary, ILM either transitions the index to a new shard allocation tier using Elasticsearch node tier labels, force-merges segments to reduce overhead, or snapshots and unmounts the index as a searchable snapshot backed by S3. A searchable snapshot in the frozen phase consumes no RAM on the Elasticsearch cluster and reads data directly from S3 on demand, combining cold economics with on-demand search capability.

Grafana Loki models its tiers through storage backend configuration. The hot path uses an in-process chunk cache (memcached or Redis) in front of an object store. Loki's compactor periodically merges small chunks into larger, more efficient objects and updates the TSDB index. The warm-to-cold boundary is implemented by pointing older prefixes at a cheaper S3 storage class via S3 Lifecycle rules. Loki itself does not know the storage class of each chunk — the S3 Lifecycle rule transparently moves data from S3 Standard to S3-IA to Glacier based on object age.

Grafana Tempo stores distributed traces as Parquet files in object storage. Each backend block covers a fixed time window (default two hours). Tempo's compactor merges small blocks, and the retention period is configured globally. The common pattern is to apply S3 Lifecycle rules to the Tempo bucket based on object age, transitioning the Parquet files to Glacier classes for long-term retention without paying for standard storage. A useful cost model for evaluating tier boundaries is:
where
is the average data volume in tier
and
is the unit storage cost per GB-month. Because

Architecture

attrs.alt: Retention tier flow: Ingest to Hot (7-30d) to Warm (30-90d) to Cold (90-365d) to Frozen/Deleted. Lifecycle policies automate transitions. Bottom panels show query lanes and comparative per-GB costs.

content.text: Ingest feeds into the hot tier (SSD-backed, sub-second query). Lifecycle policies — ES ILM, Loki compaction rules, S3 Lifecycle — automatically transition data to warm (HDD / S3-IA), then cold (Glacier Instant), and finally frozen archive or deletion. The bottom panels show which access pattern each tier serves and the approximate per-GB cost differential.
When to Use It

Tiered retention is appropriate for any production observability system that generates more than a few gigabytes of data per day and must balance real-time query performance against multi-month or multi-year data retention. The threshold is typically reached when a single data stream grows fast enough that keeping 30 days on hot storage would cost more than the engineering effort to configure lifecycle policies.

Compliance requirements make tiering mandatory rather than optional. Any system subject to SOC 2 Type II (90-day log retention minimum), HIPAA (six-year audit log minimum), PCI-DSS (one-year log retention), or similar frameworks needs at least one cold tier to hold the compliance data affordably.
When Not to Use It
Very small data volumes: if the total retained data fits on a single SSD node and the cost is negligible, the operational complexity of lifecycle policies outweighs the savings
Frequent historical access patterns: if engineering teams routinely query 180-day-old traces for feature development, placing that data in Glacier adds retrieval friction that destroys productivity
Regulatory deletion mandates: if GDPR or similar requires permanent deletion within 30 days, cold archival is counterproductive — the data should simply be deleted, not moved to a cheaper tier
How to Implement
Implementation divides into three parallel tracks: configuring Elasticsearch ILM for log indices, configuring Loki for tiered chunk storage, and applying S3 Lifecycle rules for Tempo trace backends.
Elasticsearch ILM Policy

Define an ILM policy with four phases. The hot phase applies to freshly written indices. When the index reaches a rollover condition (age or size), ILM moves it to the warm phase on HDD-allocated nodes and force-merges segments. At 30 days, ILM transitions to the cold phase using a searchable snapshot: the index is snapshotted to S3, mounted as a read-only searchable snapshot on the Elasticsearch cluster, and the local shard files are deleted. At 365 days, the snapshot is deleted.
Loki Storage and Retention Configuration

Loki stores chunks in an object store backend. The hot path uses a Redis or memcached chunk cache. The Loki compactor periodically merges small chunks and updates the TSDB index. S3 Lifecycle rules on the bucket handle the warm-to-cold transition transparently. Per-tenant retention overrides let different tenants have different retention windows from a single Loki cluster.

# loki-config.yaml — storage and retention configuration
schema_config:
  configs:
    - from: 2024-01-01
      store: tsdb
      object_store: s3
      schema: v13
      index:
        prefix: loki_index_
        period: 24h

storage_config:
  tsdb_shipper:
    active_index_directory: /var/loki/index
    cache_location: /var/loki/index_cache
    shared_store: s3
  aws:
    s3: s3://us-east-1/loki-chunks
  chunk_cache_config:
    redis:
      endpoint: redis:6379
      expiration: 168h  # 7 days in hot cache

compactor:
  working_directory: /var/loki/compactor
  shared_store: s3
  compaction_interval: 10m
  retention_enabled: true
  retention_delete_delay: 2h
  retention_delete_worker_count: 150

limits_config:
  retention_period: 365d  # global default

# loki-overrides.yaml — per-tenant retention
overrides:
  tenant-dev-001:
    retention_period: 336h    # 14 days for dev
  tenant-finserv-001:
    retention_period: 61320h  # 7 years for compliance
  tenant-security:
    retention_period: 8760h   # 365 days for SOC 2
S3 Lifecycle Rules for Warm and Cold Tiers

Both Loki and Tempo write to S3. An S3 Lifecycle policy moves objects between storage classes based on age, regardless of which application wrote them. The policy below applies to Loki chunk buckets and Tempo trace buckets and transitions through Standard (hot), S3-IA (warm), and Glacier Instant Retrieval (cold).
Tempo Compaction and Retention

# tempo-config.yaml
compactor:
  compaction:
    block_retention: 336h    # 14 days Tempo hot path
    compacted_block_retention: 1h
    compaction_window: 1h
    max_compaction_objects: 6000000
    retention_concurrency: 10
    # Blocks older than block_retention are deleted from Tempo's
    # managed index. Raw Parquet files remain in S3 and are governed
    # by the bucket Lifecycle rule above (30d -> S3-IA, 90d -> Glacier IR).

storage:
  trace:
    backend: s3
    s3:
      bucket: tempo-traces
      region: us-east-1
    pool:
      max_workers: 400
      queue_depth: 20000
Examples
Example 1: Querying Across Tiers in Loki

Loki's LogQL query language is tier-transparent: the same query syntax works against hot cache, S3 Standard, S3-IA, and Glacier IR. The difference is latency. Operators should understand which tier a given time range hits and set expectations for query response time accordingly.

# Hot tier: last 6 hours — served from Redis chunk cache or S3 Standard
# Expected latency: < 1 second
logcli query \
  '{app="agent-worker", env="prod"} |= "tool_call_failed"' \
  --from="2026-07-19T06:00:00Z" \
  --to="2026-07-19T12:00:00Z"

# Warm tier: 45 days ago — served from S3-IA
# Expected latency: 2–8 seconds first byte
logcli query \
  '{app="agent-worker", env="prod"} | json | level="error"' \
  --from="2026-06-04T00:00:00Z" \
  --to="2026-06-05T00:00:00Z"

# Cold tier: 120 days ago — served from Glacier Instant Retrieval
# Expected latency: 5–30 seconds first byte
logcli query \
  '{app="agent-worker", env="prod"} | json | trace_id="abc123"' \
  --from="2026-03-20T00:00:00Z" \
  --to="2026-03-21T00:00:00Z"
Example 2: Cost Model Comparing Tier Policies

# Compare cost of all-hot storage vs. tiered policy for 50 GB/day
TIER_COST = {    # USD per GB-month (approximate)
    "hot":  0.023,   # SSD Elasticsearch
    "warm": 0.0125,  # S3-IA
    "cold": 0.004,   # Glacier Instant Retrieval
}

def monthly_cost(gb_per_day, hot_days, warm_days, cold_days):
    avg_hot  = gb_per_day * hot_days
    avg_warm = gb_per_day * warm_days
    avg_cold = gb_per_day * cold_days
    return {
        "hot":  round(avg_hot  * TIER_COST["hot"],  2),
        "warm": round(avg_warm * TIER_COST["warm"], 2),
        "cold": round(avg_cold * TIER_COST["cold"], 2),
        "total": round(
            avg_hot  * TIER_COST["hot"] +
            avg_warm * TIER_COST["warm"] +
            avg_cold * TIER_COST["cold"], 2),
    }

# All hot for 180 days
all_hot = monthly_cost(50, 180, 0, 0)
print("All-hot 180d:", all_hot["total"], "$/month")
# => All-hot 180d: 207.0 $/month

# Tiered: 7d hot, 23d warm, 150d cold
tiered = monthly_cost(50, 7, 23, 150)
print("Tiered 7/23/150d:", tiered["total"], "$/month")
# => Tiered 7/23/150d: 44.38 $/month
# Savings: ~79%
Real-World Example

A financial services firm operates an agentic AI platform that processes trade confirmations, regulatory filings, and client communications. The platform generates approximately 500 GB of structured log data and 200 GB of distributed trace data per day. FINRA Rule 4370 and SEC 17a-4 require certain records to be retained for three to seven years, tamper-evident and immediately retrievable for the first two years.

Their implementation uses a four-tier design. Hot storage (0–14 days) uses Elasticsearch on SSD-backed i3en nodes for sub-second log search during production support. Warm storage (14–90 days) transitions to Elasticsearch warm nodes backed by st1 HDD volumes, with searchable snapshots mirrored to S3 Standard. Cold storage (90–730 days, two years) uses Elasticsearch frozen tier backed by S3 Standard-IA for the immediate-retrieval requirement. Frozen archive (two to seven years) uses S3 Glacier Deep Archive with a 12-hour retrieval time, acceptable for regulatory audit purposes. Total storage cost for this design runs approximately $18,000/month across all tiers versus an estimated $340,000/month if all data remained on hot SSD — a 19x reduction.

An ILM policy automates all transitions. A separate compliance verification job runs nightly, querying the Elasticsearch ILM API to confirm that no required-retention index has moved to the delete phase before its legal expiry. Alerts fire if any index's current phase does not match expectations for its age.
Common Mistakes
Transitioning Loki chunks to Glacier before compaction is complete: incomplete chunks moved to cold storage create queries that return partial results silently

Ignoring Glacier minimum storage duration: S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval bills a minimum 90-day storage fee; objects deleted within 90 days incur a prorated early-deletion charge that can exceed hot-tier cost

Misaligning Tempo retention with S3 Lifecycle: if Tempo's block_retention deletes blocks at 14 days but the S3 rule transitions at 30 days, objects remain on S3 Standard for 16 extra days after Tempo's tombstone
No alerting on ILM policy failure: if a warm-tier node becomes unhealthy and allocation cannot be satisfied, ILM silently stalls; hot nodes fill until disk capacity exhaustion

One retention period for all data: security audit logs (SOC 2: 90 days minimum), application debug logs (no minimum; 14–30 days is typically enough), and compliance records (years) should have separate lifecycle policies, not a single blanket duration

Assuming cold-tier queries are synchronous: Glacier Flexible Retrieval takes 3–5 hours and Deep Archive takes up to 12 hours; applications and dashboards that assume synchronous access will time out or return empty results
Best Practices

Align hot-tier duration to your P99 incident time-to-resolution: if incidents are typically resolved within 72 hours, a 7-day hot tier provides ample buffer; extending it to 30 days for peace of mind adds significant cost for rarely accessed data

Use Elasticsearch searchable snapshots for the warm phase: this avoids the cost of keeping live Elasticsearch shards for warm data — searchable snapshots read from S3 on demand, consuming no persistent disk on the cluster
Tag all S3 objects with data classification and retention class at write time: this enables targeted Lifecycle rules and simplifies compliance audits

Monitor tier transition lag: alert if the ratio of warm-to-hot data diverges from expected based on the configured transition age; large surplus data stuck in hot is a signal that lifecycle transitions are stalling

Separate compliance data from operational data at the stream or index level: this enables different lifecycle policies per stream and avoids accidentally applying a short operational retention to data with a legal hold requirement
Test cold-tier retrieval drills quarterly: confirm the retrieval workflow, latency, and data completeness for cold and frozen tiers before a real incident requires them
Performance and Scalability

Hot-tier query performance degrades when index sizes grow unbounded. Each Elasticsearch shard consumes heap memory proportional to its segment count; a cluster with 10,000 shards will run out of heap long before disk. ILM's rollover condition — rolling to a new index after 7 days or 50 GB — keeps shard count bounded. A 30-day retention window with daily rollover creates 30 hot indices and approximately 60 shards (assuming 2 primary plus 1 replica per index), a manageable cluster load.

Loki scales by sharding the write path across ingesters and distributing S3 object reads across queriers. The bottleneck for cold-tier queries is S3 object GET throughput: a single Loki querier can saturate an S3 prefix's request rate on large historical queries. Loki's query-frontend splits large time-range queries into parallel sub-queries, each issued against a different time window, which distributes the S3 load.

For Tempo, the critical performance metric is block compaction throughput. Uncompacted blocks (ingester WAL flushes) are small and numerous, causing high per-object S3 request overhead on queries. The Tempo compactor should run continuously and be sized to compact faster than data arrives. A compaction lag — where compaction cannot keep up with ingest — will visibly degrade query performance even on the hot tier.
Tooling and Ecosystem

Elasticsearch ILM: built-in lifecycle policy engine supporting hot, warm, cold, frozen, and delete phases; searchable snapshots provide cost-efficient frozen-tier query without maintaining live shards
Grafana Loki compactor: handles chunk compaction, TSDB index updates, and per-tenant retention enforcement; works against any S3-compatible backend including MinIO and GCS
Grafana Tempo compactor: merges Parquet trace blocks and enforces global retention; relies on S3 Lifecycle for cold and frozen cost management
AWS S3 Lifecycle: applies storage class transitions and expiration rules based on object age or prefix; free to configure, charges only for storage and retrieval
OpenSearch ISM (Index State Management): equivalent to Elasticsearch ILM for OpenSearch clusters; supports rollover, replica reduction, force-merge, snapshot, and delete actions
Datadog Archive and Rehydration: archives ingested logs to customer-owned S3 or GCS and provides on-demand rehydration for querying cold data in Datadog's UI
Comparison with Alternatives

Security and Reliability

Cold and frozen tier data must be protected against both accidental deletion and tampering. S3 Object Lock in compliance mode prevents objects from being deleted or overwritten for a specified retention period — a technical control that satisfies SEC 17a-4's WORM requirement. Once locked, not even the AWS account root user can delete the object before the lock expires. ILM delete actions will fail on locked objects, which is the correct behaviour: the compliance lock overrides the operational lifecycle policy.

Encrypt all tiers at rest using server-side encryption with customer-managed keys (SSE-KMS). Per-bucket KMS keys enable cryptographic erasure of a complete data set by deleting the key — relevant if personal data must be purged from the cold tier to satisfy a GDPR erasure request when individual object deletion from Glacier is impractical.

Monitor ILM and Loki retention job failures with dedicated alerts. An ILM policy that stalls in the warm phase due to insufficient disk on warm nodes will cascade: hot nodes fill with indices that cannot roll over, and ingest begins to fail. Alert on elasticsearch_ilm_explain_lifecycle_error_count greater than zero and elasticsearch_indices_shards_allocated_percent greater than 90 to catch this early.

Validate cross-region replication for compliance buckets. S3 Cross-Region Replication copies objects to a secondary region in near real-time, providing geographic redundancy for regulatory evidence. Replicate compliance-tier buckets to a second region with S3 Object Lock enabled on the replica as well, so a regional outage does not create an evidentiary gap.
Mental Model

Think of retention tiers as a hospital filing system. Patient charts from this week sit in the nurses' station for immediate access (hot tier). Charts from the past year sit in the ward filing cabinets (warm tier). Charts from years past are boxed in a records room down the hall (cold tier). Charts older than seven years are in a remote storage warehouse (frozen). A nurse never keeps all charts on the station desk — that would be impossibly crowded. Retrieving a warehouse chart takes longer, but the information is still there when the rare need arises. The hospital does not discard old charts just to clear space; it moves them to cheaper, slower storage while guaranteeing retrieval when legally required.
Key Takeaways

Hot (7–30 days), warm (30–90 days), cold (90–365 days), and frozen (1–7 years) tiers differ primarily in cost per GB and retrieval latency; the right boundary for each tier is determined by actual query access patterns, not arbitrary defaults

Elasticsearch ILM, Loki's compactor with per-tenant overrides, and S3 Lifecycle rules are the primary automation mechanisms; they operate independently and must be configured coherently to avoid data landing in the wrong tier

Compliance requirements — SOC 2 (90 days), PCI-DSS (1 year), HIPAA (6 years), SEC 17a-4 (3–7 years) — dictate minimum retention windows that cold and frozen tiers satisfy at a fraction of hot-tier cost

Moving data to Glacier saves storage cost but introduces retrieval latency (milliseconds for Glacier Instant, hours for Glacier Deep Archive); teams must know which tier holds their data before assuming a query will return quickly
S3 Object Lock in compliance mode provides WORM guarantees required by SEC 17a-4 and similar regulations; it overrides ILM delete actions, which is by design — the compliance lock wins
Alert on ILM stalls, Loki compactor lag, and unexpected hot-tier volume growth; silent failures leave data on expensive hot storage indefinitely and can eventually cause ingest failures as disk fills

Glacier's minimum 90-day billing period means premature transitions (before day 30) can cost more than staying on S3 Standard; model the full cost including retrieval fees before choosing transition boundaries
